(Internal) Map Data Scripting

#wip

Warning

This page is currently malformed. Consider editing this page to improve its formatting.

Abstract.

Colourspace formats are raster images by which data is standardised between datasets and mapped onto the game. This allows for neat data wrangling and scripting between multiple files (e.g. for automatically populating province growth rate via HYDE3.3 or for dealing with regional economic data). Such Economic Development data includes NASA’s G-Econ, though only for the years 1990-2005 at 5-year intervals. A proxy model using HYDE plus nighttime lights along the model of (Wu et al. 2024) may also be used. 

Alternatively, per capita proxy models utilising Maddison Project extrapolation can be utilised historically, but this is of much more questionable quality then even HYDE3.3’s proxy model, which still requires manual touch-ups. The intersection of the base province map and a data colourspace map (e.g. a Percentage Map) can then be used to assign sum values to individual provinces, which can then be manipulated for in-game data population. For European regional economic data per NUTS region from 1900-2015, see the Rosés-Wolf database. Extrapolation for the years following World War II are given here.

These colourspace formats are given as follows:

Percentage Maps.

A percentage map for 1950 population counts per cell, HYDE3.3, Equirectangular.

Percentage maps are standardised data maps where the highest value is represented by g = 200 ([0, 200, 0, 255]), and the lowest value by g = 0 ([0, 0, 0, 255]). Empty cells have an RGBA value of [0, 0, 0, 0]. This means that every g increment represents 0,5% of the maximum cell value. Math.round() is advised to avoid unnecessary losses in data fidelity.

Provinces (RGB).

The province map for the base game, Miller Cylindrical.

The Province colourspace stores individual Province IDs, given by the following equation:

Province ID=65536r+256g+b. 

The alpha channel is reserved for transparency, though it can be expanded later on to allow for additional data if needed. This can also be referred to as the base map as it contains the base province files that all other maps need to have a standardised resolution and projection to.

By default, the basegame projection is Miller Cylindrical. Conversely, most datasets are given in Equirectangular format. The conversion function from Equirectangular to Miller is given by the equation:

x=λ, y=54ln(tan(4)+25)), where x references the raster X, y references the raster Y, λ references the longitude in radians, and φ references the latitude in radians. Equirectangular format is simply given by x=λ, y=, so there is no need for initial transformation.
